essay_text:
When I started interning at the PA Biotech Center for the first time, I had no idea what to expect. But walking out
after meeting my mentor, I thought to myself, ‘I’ve done research projects before, this is going to be easy.’ My first
task was to make a hypothesis and outline around the project I had chosen: MRSA, an incurable bacterium that
holds potential for becoming one of the worst plagues of the 21st century.
It was when I sat down in front of my laptop at home that I realized I had no idea how to do any of those things.
The only research that I’d really done was mostly surface level, like looking up ‘plant cells’ and choosing the first
few references I saw on google. The hypotheses I’d made before mostly went like, ‘I hypothesize that plants will
grow better with more sunlight.’ Of course, such questions already had an answer going into the project, and the
idea of forming a hypothesis without already knowing the solution confounded me. In a few minutes I realized I
was completely lost. I soon found myself browsing Twitter aimlessly while trying to simultaneously come up with
an idea of what to do while repeatedly failing at doing so. I waded through endless series of Wikipedia articles and
YouTube videos on topics I couldn’t even fathom, like ‘What is a protein pathway?’ and ‘What function do proteins
serve within cells?’
Eventually, I had to decide between asking for help, and looking up random things online in order to figure out
what I was supposed to do in order to do ‘research’. I ended up sending my AP Chemistry teacher a desperate
email for assistance at two am in the morning. He happened to be awake at the time, and he was able to provide
me with a wealth of information to work off. Three hours of hard breakless work later I found myself with a
hypothesis, outline, and a migraine.
Over the year, I studied multiple different proteins in MRSA so I could locate a way to neutralize their function
within the bacteria. I was able to compete with my project in several research competitions, and I did well,
receiving various scholarships and awards. Well, until COVID caused every state competition to be canceled. I
found myself dejected, and thought my research ended up being completely pointless. After the days spent poring
over data charts, the careful creation of an experiment, and the intensive analysis done, I felt that I had lost so
much.
In the first months of quarantine I thought my research was a complete waste of time because it wasn’t judged to
be ‘good’ by upper level judges. But then I realized that I had started to put more value in the rewards for research
rather than the research itself, so I pushed myself to purge that mindset and familiarize myself with contemporary
research. As an official alumnus of the lab, I was able to access multiple scientific studies and journals during
quarantine. Coupled with my insight into the research process, I was able to understand the steps being taken by
scientists to find cures for COVID. I remember newspapers ‘predicting’ a vaccine in mere months. But I knew that
it wouldn’t happen because all research follows a similar path. At first, I thought it took a few months of google
searches and lab tests, basically your average high school project. The people expecting a cure so early think the
same as I once did, but now I have realized the truth is different. Research is a process with multiple steps from
forming a hypothesis to collaborating with others to performing data analysis—every step is vital. Because of this
knowledge, I predict a vaccine to arrive by November at the earliest and December at the latest. But this statement
could be wrong, for it is a hypothesis.
8
prompt: Discuss an accomplishment, event, or realization that sparked a period of personal growth and a new understanding of yourself or others.
persona_prompt: You are a writer who crafts narratives of personal growth through challenge. Maintain a reflective and earnest tone, using accessible language to explain complex ideas or personal revelations. Structure your writing chronologically, often contrasting initial naive assumptions with hard-earned insights. Weave in moments of self-deprecation and direct self-assessment, presenting yourself as a diligent learner who evolves through experience.
sentence_style: Varied sentence lengths, often combining descriptive clauses with shorter, direct statements for impact. Uses simple conjunctions to link ideas, creating a smooth, conversational flow. Occasional rhetorical questions are used to highlight confusion or learning.
vocabulary_level: Moderate — Uses generally accessible language but incorporates specific technical terms (e.g., MRSA, protein pathway, hypothesis) within a broader narrative, often to underscore the writer's evolving understanding. Word choice is precise without being overly academic.
structural_patterns: Chronological narrative arc, often beginning with an anecdote of initial overconfidence or naiveté, moving through a period of struggle and learning, and culminating in a significant personal realization or refined understanding. Employs contrast (past vs. present understanding, outcome vs. process) as a key structural device.
distinctive_moves: Directly quotes internal thoughts or questions to illustrate personal struggle.
Employs self-deprecating humor or honest admission of past ignorance.
Contrasts superficial initial understanding with deeper, hard-won insights.
Concludes with a broader, reflective generalization drawn from personal experience.
themes_and_preoccupations: The true nature and rigor of scientific research.
The value of process and intellectual curiosity over external validation.
Growth from naiveté to informed understanding.
Resilience in the face of setbacks.
self_presentation: Begins as naive and slightly overconfident, transitions to vulnerable and struggling, then becomes diligent and intellectually humble, ultimately presenting as analytical, insightful, and mature. Shows significant personal evolution.
cultural_or_contextual_markers: Rooted in a high school student's experience with scientific internships, AP courses, and the impact of global events (COVID-19), reflecting a typical modern Gen Z learning journey (e.g., Google/Wikipedia research, social media browsing).
avoid_in_imitation: Overly complex or academic jargon without contextual explanation.
An overtly boastful or purely self-congratulatory tone.
Ambiguous or vague descriptions of internal states or challenges.
sample_sentence: I once thought building a robot was just about snapping together parts, but after countless failed circuits and late nights troubleshooting code, I realized the real innovation lay in the patient, iterative dance of design and failure.
